text.title: Scope Management Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the scope-management methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Write exclusions before inclusions in every scope statement.
rule.rationale: Explicit 'not in scope' prevents 80% of scope disputes; ambiguity resolves toward whatever the stakeholder assumed.

rule.statement: Every must-have requirement needs a verifiable, non-tautological acceptance criterion before baselining.
rule.rationale: Requirements without AC are unverifiable; tautological ACs destroy the gate.

rule.statement: Baseline scope at end of planning; every subsequent change goes through Change Control with cost/schedule/risk delta.
rule.rationale: Without a baseline, every change becomes a negotiation.

rule.statement: Store the RTM as YAML or CSV in source control, not as a Word document.
rule.rationale: Word-doc RTMs become orphaned after sprint 2; source control gives diffs and survives tool migrations.

rule.statement: Apply a quota: no more than 60% of requirements may be 'Must'.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, stakeholders mark everything Must and scope becomes uncontrollable.

rule.statement: A Must requirement must never have status 'Won't'.
rule.rationale: This combination means the project will fail by definition; force a sponsor decision.

rule.statement: Freeze canonical requirement text and require diff-based edits; agents must not regenerate from scratch.
rule.rationale: Regeneration produces new IDs and wording — breaking all downstream traceability links.

list.item: Continuous-discovery agile product — use rolling outcomes instead of scope baselines
Pure research / spike work where scope is the question
Internal tools with fewer than 10 users where formal sign-off is theater
Crisis incident response — scope is 'stop the bleeding'
